---
title: "Working honestly with AI"
---

# Working honestly with AI

::: {.callout-warning}
## Placeholder chapter
Stub. To be drafted via the chapter-writing skill.
:::

## What this chapter will cover

A practical chapter on academic integrity, attribution, and disclosure. Not a lecture; a working guide.

- The course rule, recapped: use AI as your assistant, not your ghostwriter. You must be able to defend every line.
- What goes in a method section when AI helped: which model, which prompts (in spirit, not verbatim), what AI did, what you did, what you checked.
- What goes in a citation when AI is a real input — the current consensus is forming; we summarise the cleanest versions (APA, Chicago, journal-specific notes).
- The difference between *AI helped me write the paragraph* and *AI wrote the paragraph and I pasted it*. Both happen; only one is acceptable in academic work.
- Group projects: how to keep the line clear when one teammate over-delegates.
- A short, honest section on what your instructors are likely to detect and how.

## Why this chapter is here, near the end

You need to have used AI seriously before the integrity questions become real. By Part IX you have done seven parts of work; the rules now have something to stick to.

## TODO for the author

- A model paragraph: "AI use" disclosure for a project methods section.
- One para on the difference between *transparency about tooling* (good, expected) and *attribution of authorship* (different question, narrower).
- A note on AI-generated images, AI-generated code comments, and other places where attribution slips through the cracks.
